articles.headline: Your Cells' Power Plants Are Getting Lazy. Scientists Found How to Recharge Them.
articles.body: Here's the cruel twist of aging: your cells lose their ability to produce energy, leaving you feeling drained. Scientists at the Leibniz Institute on Aging just cracked why. A membrane lipid called phosphatidylcholine—the stuff that keeps mitochondria functioning—declines with age. When they topped up this nutrient in aging organisms, the mitochondria bounced back. They became more stable, energy production improved, and aging itself seemed reversible. The kicker: you might get it through diet or supplements. This isn't the cure-all yet, but it shows that cellular aging isn't written in stone.
articles.takeaway: Maintaining phosphatidylcholine levels through diet or supplements may help preserve mitochondrial function and slow cellular aging.

product.name: Thorne Creatine Monohydrate
product.blurb: The most researched supplement in history. Works for strength, brain health, and endurance.

articles.topic: Exercise mental cognition chemo brain cancer
articles.headline: A Daily Walk Sharpened Cancer Patients' Minds During Chemotherapy
articles.body: Chemo brain is real. Up to 80% of people undergoing chemotherapy experience mental fog, memory slips, and trouble concentrating. A new Phase III trial tested whether a simple home-based exercise program could help. Patients received resistance bands, a pedometer, and a walking plan. The results were clear: those who stuck with daily walking and resistance exercises showed better attention, faster mental processing, and fewer cognitive complaints than those who didn't exercise. Family and friends noticed the difference too. No fancy gym. No expensive equipment. Just movement, done regularly.
articles.takeaway: Daily walking and light resistance training can reduce chemo brain symptoms and help maintain mental clarity during cancer treatment.

product.name: TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller
product.blurb: Five minutes of rolling saves hours of stiffness. Non-negotiable on hard training days.

articles.topic: Slow breathing vagus nerve stress paramedic students
articles.headline: Five Breaths Per Minute Stopped Paramedic Stress Dead in Its Tracks
articles.body: Paramedic students face brutal exam pressure. A randomized trial gave some of them a structured breathing protocol: slow, diaphragmatic breathing at about five breaths per minute, done twice daily. The mechanism is pure physiology. Slow breathing activates your vagus nerve, increases heart-rate variability, and shifts your nervous system from fight-or-flight to rest-and-digest. The control group's stress spiked as exams approached. The breathwork group's didn't. They stayed calm while their peers spiraled. The study adds weight to a growing body of 2026 research showing that protocolized breathwork isn't just wellness noise—it's a deployable mental-health intervention.
articles.takeaway: Structured diaphragmatic breathing at five breaths per minute can buffer stress and prevent anxiety spikes during high-pressure periods.

product.name: Why We Sleep - Matthew Walker Audiobook
product.blurb: Read this and you will never deprioritize sleep again.

articles.headline: The Biggest Collagen Study Ever Shows It Actually Works. Here's How Long You Need.
articles.body: Collagen supplements fill store shelves. Do they work? Scientists analyzed 113 clinical trials covering nearly 8,000 people and finally delivered a clear answer: yes, but with caveats. Collagen improved skin elasticity, hydration, and joint function in people with osteoarthritis. The catch? Results built gradually. Taking it consistently for eight to twelve weeks matters way more than a short burst. You also got modest benefits for muscle and tendon health, which matters for aging well. Collagen isn't a panacea, but if you're over 40 and dealing with stiff joints or aging skin, the evidence supports it.
articles.takeaway: Consistent collagen supplementation for 8-12+ weeks improves skin elasticity and reduces pain in osteoarthritis, but requires ongoing use.
articles.source: Aesthetic Surgery Journal Open Forum / ScienceDaily

product.name: Pure Encapsulations Magnesium Glycinate
product.blurb: The most underused sleep and recovery tool. Most athletes are deficient.

quote.text: I'm brilliant at pushing myself, but often less good at slowing down. In 2026, I want to be intentional about sleep, stretching, and taking rest without guilt – because that's when the real adaptation happens.
quote.author: Helen Glover
quote.context: British Olympic rower and multi-medal champion athlete on prioritizing recovery alongside training.

workout.title: Kettlebell Swing & Burpee Sprint: Work-Rest Metabolic Density
workout.tip: Kettlebell swings are hip-driven, not arm-driven—load your glutes at the bottom and explode through the hips. Rest periods are just as important as work: use them to catch your breath and reset posture before the next round.
workout.warmup: 5 min: 10 cat-cows, 10 glute bridges, 8 kettlebell figure-8s each direction, 5 light kettlebell swings, 5 burpees at 50% intensity

exercises.name: Kettlebell Swing (Double)
exercises.notes: 40 sec work / 40 sec rest. Hip hinge with flat back, drive through heels, explosive hip snap at top. Perfect form over speed—loose swings kill fat loss gains.

exercises.name: Burpee Broad Jump
exercises.notes: 40 sec work / 40 sec rest. Full push-up + broad jump each rep. Land soft, reset posture before next rep. These build explosive lower-body power essential for Hyrox sled work.

workout.cooldown: 5 min: walking in place 1 min, quad stretch 30 sec each leg, forward fold 45 sec, child's pose 30 sec, deep breathing
workout.description: A pure metabolic conditioning circuit alternating explosive kettlebell swings with burpee broad jumps in 1:1 work-to-rest intervals. This session builds posterior chain power, cardiovascular capacity, and fat loss through compound movement complexity without requiring a barbell or rack setup.

meal.title: Teriyaki Salmon Onigiri (Japanese Rice Balls)
meal.instructions:
1. Pan-sear salmon over medium heat for 5–6 minutes per side until cooked through; flake into small pieces.
2. In a small bowl, mix soy sauce and mirin; toss flaked salmon in the glaze.
3. Divide warm sushi rice into two portions (makes 2 rice balls).
4. Press one portion of rice into a wet hand, create a small indent, fill with 1 tbsp teriyaki salmon, top with another layer of rice, and gently shape into a ball.
5. Repeat with second portion.
6. Sprinkle with sesame seeds and a pinch of salt.
meal.tip: Flaked teriyaki-glazed salmon folded into warm sushi rice and shaped into portable rice balls—an authentic, carb-rich pre-workout fuel with 30g protein that travels well and sits light in your stomach before a brick session or long run.
